chunk_summary: Guidelines for evaluating plant-based beverages as milk substitutes in toddlers, emphasizing nutritional adequacy, label checks, and professional supervision for vegan or allergy-constrained families.
learning_objectives: Understand why plant beverages are unsuitable as milk substitutes for infants (0–12 months).
Learn how to evaluate plant beverage labels for protein, fortification, and hidden sugars.
Identify safe substitution strategies for toddlers in vegan or allergy-constrained families.
Recognize the risks of excessive plant beverage consumption in toddlers.
key_facts: Plant beverages are not nutritionally equivalent to breastmilk or infant formula for infants (0–12 months).
Fortified soy beverages are typically the closest substitute for toddlers, but brands vary widely in protein content.
Label checklist includes confirming unsweetened products, meaningful protein per serving, calcium and vitamin D fortification, and vitamin B12 for vegan families.
Excessive plant beverage consumption can displace meals and worsen growth faltering.
Low-protein beverages like almond or oat drinks are unsuitable as milk substitutes without additional protein anchors.
reference_quote: Do not use plant beverages as milk substitutes in infancy (0–12 months). Infants should receive breastmilk or appropriate infant formula; plant beverages are not nutritionally equivalent and can create protein, fat, and micronutrient gaps.
document_key_topics: Risks of excessive plant beverage consumption
